14                          I.    INTRODUCTION                             
15        Plaintiff Glen Littleton filed a securities class action against Defendants Tesla, Inc.; Elon 
16   Musk (Tesla’s CEO and former Chairman); and Tesla’s Board of Directors based on two tweets 
17   made by Mr. Musk in August 2018 about taking Tesla from a public to a private company.  After a 
18   three-week trial in January 2023, the jury awarded a verdict for Defendants on all claims.   
19        Now pending before the Court are (1) Plaintiff’s motion for judgment as a matter of law or, 
20   in the alternative, for a new trial, see Docket No. 685, and (2) supplemental briefing from the 
21   parties regarding whether the Court shall award costs to Defendants as the prevailing party under 
22   Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 54.  See Docket Nos. 688; 689.  As set forth below, the Court 
23   DENIES Plaintiff’s motion for judgment as a matter of law or for a new trial because Plaintiff 
24   failed to move on essential elements and because substantial evidence demonstrates that the tweets 
25   at issue were not material.  Plaintiff is not entitled to judgment as a matter of law with respect to 
26   materiality or reliance.  Plaintiff has, however, rebutted Rule 54(d)’s presumption that he should 
27   pay Defendants’ costs as the prevailing party.  Each party shall pay their own costs.    
1                          II.   BACKGROUND                               
2   A.   Plaintiff’s Claims                                               
3        Plaintiff Glen Littleton represents a class of individuals and entities who purchased or sold 
4   Tesla stock, options, and other securities from 9:48 a.m. PST on August 7, 2018 to August 17, 
5   2018 (the “Class Period”).  Plaintiff brought claims against Defendants Elon Musk and Tesla for 
6   violations of Sections 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (15 U.S.C. §§ 78j, 78t) and 
7   SEC Rule 10b-5(b) (17 C.F.R. § 240.10b-5) when Mr. Musk published from his personal Twitter 
8   account two tweets on August 7, 2018: “Am considering taking Tesla private at $420. Funding 
9   secured” and “Investor support is confirmed. Only reason why this is not certain is that it’s 
10   contingent on a shareholder vote.”1  Plaintiff also brought a Section 20(a) claim against all 
11   members of Tesla’s Board of Directors on the grounds that they were controlling persons of Tesla. 
12   B.   Summary Judgment Order                                           
13        On April 1, 2022, the Court granted partial summary judgment to Plaintiff on the issues of 
14   falsity and scienter.  Docket No. 387 (Summary Judgment Order).  Specifically, the Court found 
15   that the following statements in the Musk Tweets were false and made recklessly: “Funding 
16   secured” and “Investor support is confirmed. Only reason why this is not certain is that it’s 
17   contingent on a shareholder vote.”  Id. at 24–29.  The Court declined to grant summary judgment 
18   for Plaintiff on the element of reliance under the fraud-on-the-market presumption because there 
19   were disputed issues of material fact on materiality and Defendants were entitled to rebut the 
20   fraud-on-the-market presumption.  Id. at 32.  Specifically, the Court found that “there is evidence 
21   suggesting that the misrepresentations [at issue] did not actually affect the market price” of Tesla 
22   securities because “there is evidence that, after the 8/13/2018 blog post, which served as a partial 
23   corrective disclosure, there was no decline or at least not a significant decline in stock price; thus, 
24   arguably, the reaction to the tweets on 8/7/2018 was a response to Mr. Musk contemplating taking 
25   Tesla private and not to statements that, e.g., funding was secured or investor support confirmed.”  
26

12   C.   The Trial                                                        
13        On January 17, 2023, the parties went to trial on Plaintiff’s claims. At trial, Plaintiff bore 
14   the burden to prove each element of his Rule 10b-5 claim against Defendants Elon Musk and 
15   Tesla: (1) that Elon Musk and/or Tesla made untrue statements of a material fact in connection 
16   with the purchase or sale of securities, (2) Elon Musk and/or Tesla acted with the necessary state 
17   of mind, (3) Elon Musk and/or Tesla used an instrument of interstate commerce, (4) Plaintiff 
18   justifiably relied on Elon Musk and/or Tesla’s untrue statements of material fact in buying or 
19   selling Tesla securities, and (5) loss causation.  Docket No. 655 (Jury Instructions) at 7.  In 
20   addition, as to Tesla, Plaintiff bore the burden to impute Mr. Musk’s state of mind to Tesla.  Id. at 
21   12.  Plaintiff also bore the burden to prove his Section 20(a) claim against each of the Director 
22   Defendants.  Id. at 18.  Consistent with the Court’s summary judgment order, the jury was 
23   instructed:                                                           

24             You are to assume that the statements ‘Funding secured’ and 
              ‘Investor support is confirmed. Only reason why this is not certain is 
25             that it’s contingent on a shareholder vote.’ were untrue.  But you 
              still must decide whether these statements were of material facts.  
26             You must also assume Mr. Musk acted with reckless disregard for 
              whether the statements were true.  But you must still decide whether 
27             he knew that the statements were untrue.

19   D.   Plaintiff’s Pre-Verdict Rule 50(a) Motion                        
20        On February 1, 2023, before the close of evidence, Plaintiff filed a Rule 50(a) motion for 
21   judgment as a matter of law with respect to two issues: (1) the Rule 10b-5 claim element of 
22   materiality, and (2) classwide and individual reliance.  Docket No. 657 (Rule 50(a) Motion) at 1–

24   elements of the claim, such as loss causation.  See, e.g., id. at ii–iii.  Nor did Plaintiff move for 
25   judgment as Tesla’s imputed liability or his Section 20(a) claim against the Director Defendants.  
26   Id.                                                                   
27   E.   Plaintiff’s Renewed Motion for Judgment as a Matter of Law       
1   the Alternative for a New Trial.  See Docket No. 685 (Renewed Motion for Judgment as a Matter 
2   of Law, or “Mot.”).  While Plaintiff indicated that he was moving for judgment as a matter of law 
3   “on the remaining 10b-5 elements” for liability, the motion is limited to the issues of materiality,

5        With respect to loss causation, Plaintiff appears to adopt two contradictory positions.  In

12   law . . . ?”).   But in the body of his motion, Plaintiff argued that the “[t]he evidence at trial firmly

15   (contending that “all elements, except materiality and reliance, are undisputed”).  As far as the 
16   Court can tell, Plaintiff argues that loss causation is (1) an undisputed element; (2) that flows from 
17   a determination of materiality; and (3) should be decided by a new jury in a damages trial. 
18        Plaintiff also moved for a new trial on damages based on three purportedly confusing jury 
19   instructions.  Mot. at 20.                                            
20                        III.  LEGAL STANDARD                             
21   A.   Motion for Judgment as a Matter of Law                           
22        Judgment as a matter of law under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 50(b) is granted “only 
23   if, under the governing law, there can be but one reasonable conclusion as to the verdict.”  Winarto 
24   v. Toshiba Am. Elecs. Components, Inc., 274 F.3d 1276, 1283 (9th Cir. 2001).  When evaluating 
25   such a motion, “the court must draw all reasonable inferences in favor of the nonmoving party, 
26   and it may not make credibility determinations or weigh the evidence.”  Reeves v. Sanderson 
27   Plumbing Prod., Inc., 530 U.S. 133, 150 (2000).  The Ninth Circuit has made clear that a court 
1   180 F.3d 997, 1012 (9th Cir. 1999).  Substantial evidence means “evidence adequate to support 
2   the jury’s conclusion, even if it is also possible to draw a contrary conclusion” from the same 
3   evidence.  Castro v. Cnty. of Los Angeles, 833 F.3d 1060, 1066 (9th Cir. 2016) (internal quotation 
4   marks omitted).  “Thus, although the court should review the record as a whole, it must disregard 
5   evidence favorable to the moving party that the jury is not required to believe, and may not 
6   substitute its view of the evidence for that of the jury.”  Reeves, 530 U.S. at 151.  In other words, 
7   entry of judgment as a matter of law is warranted only “if the evidence, construed in the light most 
8   favorable to the nonmoving party, permits only one reasonable conclusion, and that conclusion is 
9   contrary to the jury’s verdict.”  Castro, 833 F.3d at 1066 (internal quotation marks omitted). 
10   B.   Motion for a New Trial                                           
11        Under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 59(a)(1), a court “may, on motion, grant a new trial 
12   on all or some of the issues.”  Fed. R. Civ. P. 59(a).  A trial court may grant a new trial, “even 
13   though the verdict is supported by substantial evidence, if the verdict is contrary to the clear 
14   weight of the evidence, or is based upon evidence which is false, or to prevent, in the sound 
15   discretion of the trial court, a miscarriage of justice.”  United States v. 4.0 Acres of Land, 175 F.3d 
16   1133, 1139 (9th Cir. 1999).  In considering a Rule 59(a) motion, the court “is not required to view 
17   the trial evidence in the light most favorable to the verdict.  Instead, the district court can weigh 
18   the evidence and assess the credibility of the witnesses.”  Experience Hendrix L.L.C. v. 
19   Hendrixlicensing.com Ltd., 762 F.3d 829, 842 (9th Cir. 2014).         
20        The decision to grant a new trial falls within the sound discretion of the trial court.  Kode v. 
21   Carlson, 596 F.3d 608, 611 (9th Cir. 2010).  However, a court should not grant a new trial unless 
22   it is “left with the definite and firm conviction that a mistake has been committed.”  Landes 
23   Constr. Co. v. Royal Bank of Can., 833 F.2d 1365, 1372 (9th Cir. 1987) (internal quotations 
24   omitted).  A trial court’s determination that the verdict is not against the clear weight of the 
25   evidence is upheld unless there is an “absolute absence of evidence to support the jury’s verdict.”  
26   Hung Lam v. City of San Jose, 869 F.3d 1077, 1084 (9th Cir. 2017) (internal quotation marks 
27   omitted) (emphasis in original).                                      
1   C.   Costs                                                            
2        Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 54(d)(1) provides that “[u]nless a federal statute, these 
3   rules, or a court order provides otherwise, costs—other than attorney’s fees—should be allowed to 
4   the prevailing party.”  Fed. R. Civ. P. 54(d)(1).  “Rule 54(d) creates a presumption in favor of 
5   awarding costs to prevailing parties, and it is incumbent upon the losing party to demonstrate why 
6   the costs should not be awarded.”  Stanley v. Univ. of S. Cal., 178 F.3d 1069, 1079 (9th Cir. 1999). 
7        Because of Rule 54(d)’s presumption in favor of awarding costs to a prevailing party, “a 
8   district court need not give affirmative reasons for awarding costs; instead, it need only find that 
9   the reasons for denying costs are not sufficiently persuasive to overcome the presumption in favor 
10   of an award.”  Save Our Valley v. Sound Transit, 335 F.3d 932, 945 (9th Cir. 2003).  A court that 
11   declines to award costs, however, must justify its decision by explaining “why a case is not 
12   ‘ordinary’ and why, in the circumstances, it would be inappropriate or inequitable to award costs.”  
13   Ass’n of Mexican–Am. Educators v. State of Cal., 231 F.3d 572, 593 (9th Cir. 2000) (en banc); see 
14   Save Our Valley, 335 F.3d at 945 (“A district court deviates from normal practice when it refuses 
15   to tax costs to the losing party, and that deviation triggers the requirement to ‘specify reasons.’”).   
16        Appropriate reasons for denying costs include: (1) the substantial public importance of the 
17   case, (2) the closeness and difficulty of the issues in the case, (3) the chilling effect on future 
18   similar actions, (4) the plaintiff's limited financial resources, and (5) the economic disparity 
19   between the parties.  Escriba v. Foster Poultry Farms, Inc., 743 F.3d 1236, 1247–48 (9th Cir. 
20   2014) (citing Ass’n of Mex.–Am. Educators, 231 F.3d at 592–93).  This is not “an exhaustive list 
21   of ‘good reasons’ for declining to award costs,” but rather a starting point for analysis.  Id.  
22                           IV.   DISCUSSION                              
23   A.   Plaintiff’s Motion for Judgment as a Matter of Law               
24        Procedurally and substantively, Plaintiffs’ motion is flawed.  The Court begins by 
25   discussing the limited scope of Plaintiffs’ initial Rule 50(a) motion, which necessarily limits the 
26   relief that Plaintiffs are able to secure in their Rule 50(b) motion.  But as set forth below, even if 
27   Plaintiffs had sought judgment on all elements of their claims, the Court would still deny the 
1   materiality.                                                          
2        1.   The Court Cannot Enter Judgment as a Matter of Law Because Plaintiff Did Not 
3             Move on Essential Elements of his Rule 10b-5 Claim          
4        At the outset, the Court addresses a threshold procedural issue regarding the scope of the 
5   Rule 50 motion before the Court.  As Defendants note, Plaintiff moved with respect to materiality 
6   and reliance in his initial Rule 50(a) motion.  Because Plaintiff did not move on other essential 
7   elements of the claim—most particularly, loss causation—Defendants contend that the Court 
8   cannot enter judgment for Plaintiff on the Rule 10b-5 claim.  See Docket No. 791 (Opposition to 
9   Plaintiff’s JMOL, or “Opp.”) at 9–10.                                 
10        Plaintiff responds that Rule 50 permits parties to raise challenges on discrete legal issues 
11   rather than a complete legal claim.  Reply at 4.  Plaintiff is missing the point.  While a party is not 
12   required to move for judgment as a matter of law on all elements of its claims, Plaintiff’s failure to 
13   seek judgment as to each element required for his Rule 10b-5 claim necessarily limits the Court’s 
14   analysis of the claim and therefore precludes the Court from entering judgment as a matter of law.   
15        Sagicor Life Insurance Co. v. Jang is on point.  In this malicious prosecution case, the 
16   plaintiffs sought judgment as a matter of law “only on the issue of malice.”  See No. 19-cv-2028, 
17   2022 WL 17328237, at *5 (C.D. Cal. Oct. 27, 2022).  Because the plaintiffs had exclusively 
18   moved on a single element, the scope of the Rule 50(b) motion was “limited to that one issue.”  Id.  
19   The Court denied the motion because “[w]ithout reaching all elements of Plaintiffs’ claim, the 
20   Court cannot enter judgment as a matter of law.”  Id.; see also Marker v. City of San Jose, No. 09-
21   cv-05956-RMW, 2014 WL 5302175, at *1 (N.D. Cal. Oct. 16, 2014) (denying plaintiff’s motion 
22   for JMOL where substantial evidence supported jury’s verdict as to one element, even if the court 
23   presumed plaintiff would prevail on other elements).                  
24        Plaintiff must establish loss causation in order to prevail in his section 10b-5 claim.  See, 
25   e.g., Metzler Inv. GMBH v. Corinthian Colleges, Inc., 540 F.3d 1049, 1052 (9th Cir. 2008) 
26   (affirming dismissal of 10b-5 claim for failure to plead loss causation); see also Jury Instructions 
27   at 15.  Plaintiff did not seek judgment with respect to this element in his original pre-verdict 
1   sufficient basis from which the jury could have found for Defendants on the element of loss 
2   causation.  A party which has failed to establish liability is not entitled to a new trial on damages.   
3        In sum: the Court cannot enter judgment as a matter of law for Plaintiff on the Rule 10b-5 
4   claim because Plaintiff did not move with respect to loss causation.  Moreover, there was 
5   substantial evidence at trial from which the jury could have concluded that the Tweets were not 
6   material and, therefore, that the elements of materiality and reliance had not been satisfied.  
7        2.   Substantial Evidence Demonstrates that the Tweets Were Not Material 
8        Plaintiff’s motion is unavailing because substantial evidence at trial supported the 
9   conclusion that the Tweets were not material.                         
10        The question of materiality depends on how significant a misrepresented fact would be to a 
11   reasonable investor.  See TSC Indus., Inc. v. Northway, Inc., 426 U.S. 438, 445 (1976); see also 
12   Jury Instructions at 10 (defining materiality).  To establish this element, Plaintiff needed to prove 
13   both that (1) there is a “substantial likelihood that a reasonable investor would consider the 
14   [misrepresented] fact important in deciding whether to buy or sell that security” and (2) the 
15   “misrepresentation gives a reasonable investor the impression of a state of affairs that differs in a 
16   material way from the one that actually exists.”  See Jury Instructions at 10; TSC Indus., 426 U.S.

20   Sec. Litig., 126 F. Supp. 2d 1248, 1259 (N.D. Cal. 2000) (same).

22   Indus., 426 U.S. at 449 (explaining that the materiality standard requires showing “a substantial

27   circumstances under which they were made, not misleading.”) (emphasis added); see also Matrixx

13   B.   Plaintiff’s Motion for a New Trial                               
14        Plaintiff presents two arguments in support of his request for a new trial.  First, he claims 
15   that because “[t]he evidence at trial firmly supports that Musk’s tweets were material and, in turn, 
16   the elements of reliance and loss causation should be decided in Plaintiff’s favor . . . letting the 
17   jury’s verdict stand would amount to a ‘manifest miscarriage of justice.’”  Mot. at 20 (quoting 
18   EEOC v. Go Daddy Software, Inc., 581 F.3d 951, 961 (9th Cir. 2009)).  Second, he contends that a 
19   new trial is warranted because “the jury instructions created confusion amongst the jurors that led 
20   to the erroneous verdict.”  Id. (capitalization altered for clarity).  Neither argument passes muster.       
21        1.   The Verdict Was Not Against the Clear Weight of the Evidence 
22        Plaintiff’s theory that a defense verdict goes against the clear weight of the evidence rests 
23   on two incorrect premises—that the Tweets were indisputably material and that “the elements of 
24   liability” have been decided in favor of Plaintiff.  Mot. at 20.  Again, the Court has already 
25   concluded that substantial evidence demonstrates that the Tweets were not material.  See Section 
26   IV. A.2, supra.  And puzzlingly, it appears that Plaintiff assumes that the Court will decide loss 
27   causation in Plaintiff’s favor, even though Plaintiff has not presented any argument or evidence 
1   at 20.  This assumption is clearly unsupportable, not least because there was substantial evidence 
2   from which the jury could have concluded that Plaintiff had not established loss causation.   
3        For example, the jury could have found that there was insufficient evidence of loss 
4   causation due to Plaintiff’s failure to isolate the impact of the false “funding secured” statement 
5   from the true statement that Mr. Musk was considering taking Tesla private at $420.  See Jury 
6   Instructions at 15 (“Plaintiff must reasonably distinguish any security-price reaction to the 
7   misrepresentations at issue from the market’s reaction to other factors, such as other information 
8   or events that could affect the prices of Tesla’s securities.”); see also Nuveen Mun. High Income 
9   Opportunity Fund v. City of Alameda, 730 F.3d 1111, 1123 (9th Cir. 2013) (“Because there are a 
10   tangle of factors that affect [price], evidence that certain [false statements] are responsible for a 
11   loss must reasonably distinguish the impact of those [statements] from other economic factors.”).  
12   It is undisputed that Dr. Hartzmark analyzed both Tweets together as “an interwoven bundle” and 
13   did not separate out the price effect or market impact of the undisputedly true portion of Mr. 
14   Musk’s Tweet—that he was considering taking Tesla private at $420—from the alleged false 
15   statements. TT 1694:24-1695:7; TT 1732:21-1733:6.  Thus, the jury could reasonably have found 
16   that Plaintiff had not established loss causation.                    
17        Because Plaintiff has not shown that the verdict was against the clear weight of the 
18   evidence and there is no miscarriage of justice, the Court rejects Plaintiff’s first argument for a 
19   new trial.                                                            
20        2.   There Was No Instructional Error                            
21        Next, Plaintiff contends that three jury instructions “gave rise to confusion amongst the 
22   jurors and, ultimately, led to an erroneous and prejudicial verdict.”  Mot. at 20.  Plaintiff takes 
23   issue with Jury Instruction Nos. 6 and 9 and with the Court’s rejection of Plaintiff’s proposed 
24   missing witness jury instruction.  Id. at 20–25.  These arguments are meritless. 
25        “The standard of review on appeal for an alleged error in jury instructions depends on the 
26   nature of the claimed error.  When an appellant alleges an error in the formulation of the jury 
27   instructions, the instructions are considered as a whole and an abuse of discretion standard is 
1   the trial court misstated the elements that must be proved at trial, the reviewing court must view

7   jury in a civil case does not require reversal if it is harmless.”  Acosta v. City of Costa Mesa, 718 
8   F.3d 800, 827 (9th Cir. 2013).

21   that the Court’s “you are to assume” language was an “instructional error” that resulted in the 
22   Court misstating the elements that must be proved at trial.  Reply at 13.  The Court disagrees on

25   to be proved at trial.  The instruction adequately informed the jury of its charge—and what issues 
26   it had to assume were resolved—while protecting Defendants from the risk of prejudice arising 
27   from an express reference to a judicial finding of fact.  The Court has discretion in formulating

25   C.   Costs                                                            
26        The Court turns to the final issue, which is whether the Court should order Plaintiff to pay 
27   Defendants’ taxable costs as the prevailing party.  As set forth below, the Court finds that Plaintiff 
1   costs.                                                                
2        “Rule 54(d) creates a presumption in favor of awarding costs to prevailing parties, and it is 
3   incumbent upon the losing party to demonstrate why the costs should not be awarded.”  Stanley, 
4   178 F.3d at 1079 (citing National Info. Servs., Inc. v. TRW, Inc., 51 F.3d 1470, 1471–72 (9th Cir. 
5   1995)).  Appropriate reasons for denying costs include: (1) the substantial public importance of the 
6   case, (2) the closeness and difficulty of the issues in the case, (3) the chilling effect on future 
7   similar actions, (4) the plaintiff’s limited financial resources, and (5) the economic disparity 
8   between the parties.  Escriba, 743 F.3d at 1247–48 (citing Ass’n of Mex.–Am. Educators, 231 F.3d 
9   at 592–93).  This is not “an exhaustive list of ‘good reasons’ for declining to award costs,” but 
10   rather a starting point for analysis.  Id.                            
11        1.   Substantial Public Importance of the Case                   
12        When a case involves an issue of substantial public importance, it is a factor that weighs 
13   against awarding costs to the prevailing party.  Escriba, 743 F.3d at 1247–48.  There are multiple 
14   aspects to the present case which demonstrate that it involves an issue of substantial public 
15   importance.  First, according to Plaintiff, “[o]ver 22 million individuals” received the Tweets in 
16   which Mr. Musk announced that he had secured funding for the privatization transaction, and 
17   “hundreds of thousands, if not millions, of Tesla investors saw an impact to their financial 
18   portfolios.”  Docket No. 688 (Plaintiff’s Supplemental Briefing re Costs, or “Pl. Costs Br.”) at 2. 
19   Within days of the Tweets, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission promptly commenced 
20   investigations and filed lawsuits in response to Mr. Musk’s conduct, and news outlets published 
21   thousands of articles regarding the Tweets.  The barrage of media coverage leading up to and 
22   during the trial also reveals the public interest in the case.        
23        Second, this case is one of very few securities class actions to proceed to trial.  The 
24   Supreme Court has explained that “an animating purpose” of securities law is “to insure honest 
25   securities markets and thereby promote investor confidence.”  United States v. O’Hagan, 521 U.S. 
26   642, 658 (1997); see also Dura Pharm., Inc. v. Broudo, 544 U.S. 336, 345 (2005) (“The securities 
27   statutes seek to maintain public confidence in the marketplace.  They do so by deterring fraud, in 
1   According to Plaintiff, since Congress passed the PSLRA in 1995, fewer than 30 cases have ever 
2   made it to trial.  Id. at 2.  These facts also demonstrates that the case in particular involved issues 
3   of substantial public importance.                                     
4        This factor thus weighs against awarding costs to Defendants.    
5        2.   Closeness and Difficulty of the Issues in the Case          
6        The closer the case, the more it weighs against awarding costs to the prevailing party.  
7   Escriba, 743 F.3d at 1248.  In Draper, the Ninth Circuit determined the case was close for two 
8   reasons: (1) plaintiff’s evidence of an Eighth Amendment violation was sufficient to survive 
9   summary judgment, and (2) the case ultimately turned on which competing account of events the 
10   jurors believed.  Draper v. Rosario, 836 F.3d 1072, 1088 (9th Cir. 2016). 
11        Mr. Littleton’s case was close and presented difficult issues.  Last year, the Court granted 
12   partial summary judgment to Plaintiff on the issues of falsity and scienter, two elements of the 
13   Section 10b-5 claim.  See Summary Judgment Order at 32–33.  And as in Draper, the issue of 
14   materiality—one of the key issues in the case—turned in part on the jury’s assessment of witness 
15   credibility.  Draper, 836 F.3d at 1088; see also Lopez v. Nguyen, No. 13-CV-3870-CRB, 2017 
16   WL 512773, at *2 (N.D. Cal. Feb. 8, 2017) (“Mr. Lopez’s case was close because his claims 
17   survived summary judgment as to four defendants and, as in Draper, turned on which witness 
18   testimony the jury believed.”).                                       
19        This factor thus also weighs against awarding costs to Defendants. 
20        3.   Chilling Effect on Future Similar Actions                   
21        If awarding costs may chill future similar actions, that too weighs against allowing their 
22   recovery by the prevailing party.  Escriba, 743 F.3d at 1248.  Courts have found that taxing costs 
23   in labor and employment class actions would have a chilling effect on similar class actions.  See, 
24   e.g., Escriba, 743 F.3d at 1248–49 (refusing to award costs in FMLA case); Makaneole v. 
25   Solarworld Indus. Am., Inc., No. 14-cv-1528, 2017 WL 2345706, at *3 (D. Or. May 10, 2017), 
26   report and recommendation adopted, 2017 WL 2345694 (D. Or. May 30, 2017) (refusing to 
27   award costs in wage-claim litigation because of the “significant and undesirable chilling effect on 
1   4935972, at *1 (C.D. Cal. June 11, 2021) (declining to award costs in light of a chilling effect on 
2   future labor and employment class actions).                           
3        A securities fraud class action is not entirely analogous to a wage and hour class action.  
4   The high stakes and potential award may counter the chilling effect of an award of costs to the 
5   prevailing defendant.  Yet, as noted above, this case was complex and raised substantial issues.  
6   Under the circumstances of this case, this factor is neutral.         
7        4.   Financial Resources                                         
8        A party’s limited financial resources weighs against taxing costs.  Escriba, 743 F.3d at 
9   1248.  Although Plaintiff characterizes himself as a “semi-retired individual investor,” Plaintiff 
10   does not provide any specific information regarding his financial resources.  See Pl. Costs Br. at 4.  
11   Without providing information regarding Mr. Littleton’s financial situation, the Court is unable to 
12   determine whether or not paying Defendants’ costs would be a financial hardship.  See Courtney v. 
13   Oregon Dep’t of State Police, No. 06-cv-6223, 2008 WL 11408791, at *2 (D. Or. Aug. 25, 2008) 
14   (ordering costs where there was “no proof of limited resources”); cf. Escriba, 743 F.3d at 1248 
15   (declining to award costs where plaintiff “earned an average of $11,622 per year while working 
16   for Foster Farms, meaning that the costs being sought by the company exceed[ed] her average 
17   annual earnings”); Hamilton v. Yavapai Cmty. Coll. Dist., No. 12-cv-08193, 2022 WL 504474, at 
18   *2 (D. Ariz. Feb. 18, 2022) (refusing to award costs where plaintiff provided information 
19   demonstrating that his average monthly expenses exceeded his income and that his family was 
20   financially dependent on him).                                        
21        Because Mr. Littleton has not demonstrated that paying Defendants’ costs would be a 
22   financial hardship, this factor weighs in favor of awarding costs.    
23        5.   Economic Disparity Between the Parties                      
24        The final factor turns on the economic disparity among the parties.  Escriba, 743 F.3d at 
25   1248.   While an economic disparity between the parties weighs against awarding costs, “it 
26   appears to be so only where the party seeking to avoid costs has limited financial resources and 
27   thus payment of costs would present a significant hardship.”  Jardin v. DATAllegro, Inc., No. 08-
1   Plaintiff has not pointed to any authority—nor has the Court independently been able to locate 
2   any—where an economic disparity between the parties weighed against awarding costs absent a 
3   corollary finding that the party had limited financial resources (despite the logic of considering 
4   economic disparity irrespective of the plaintiffs not being impoverished).  See id. (awarding costs 
5   despite economic disparity where plaintiff “painted himself as a ‘highly successful database 
6   architecture inventor’”); cf. Lopez, 2017 WL 512773, at *3 (citing examples where a party’s 
7   limited financial resources and economic disparity both weighed against awarding costs).   
8        Because it appears that an economic disparity between the parties only weighs against 
9   awarding costs where a party has limited financial resources and Mr. Littleton has not made this 
10   showing, this final factor weighs in favor of awarding costs.         
11        In sum: of the five Escriba factors, the first and second factors weigh against awarding 
12   costs, the third is neutral, and the fourth and fifth factors weigh in favor of awarding costs.  While 
13   a somewhat close call, in light of the substantial public importance of the case and the highly 
14   disputed issues of fact (with the Court having found falsity and scienter in favor of Plaintiffs prior 
15   to trial), the Court concludes that the Escriba factors are sufficient to overcome the presumption of 
16   awarding costs against Plaintiff in this case.  Each party shall bear their own costs.   
17                           V.    CONCLUSION                              
18        For the foregoing reasons, the Court DENIES Plaintiff’s motion for judgment as a matter 
19   of law and for a new trial.  The Court ORDERS each party to bear their own costs. 
20        This order disposes of Docket Nos. 657, 685, and 689.

20                        III.  LEGAL STANDARD                             
21   A.   Motion for Judgment as a Matter of Law                           
22        Judgment as a matter of law under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 50(b) is granted “only 
23   if, under the governing law, there can be but one reasonable conclusion as to the verdict.”  Winarto 
24   v. Toshiba Am. Elecs. Components, Inc.,
274 F.3d 1276, 1283
 (9th Cir. 2001).  When evaluating 
25   such a motion, “the court must draw all reasonable inferences in favor of the nonmoving party, 
26   and it may not make credibility determinations or weigh the evidence.”  Reeves v. Sanderson 
27   Plumbing Prod., Inc.,
530 U.S. 133, 150
 (2000).  The Ninth Circuit has made clear that a court 
1
180 F.3d 997, 1012
 (9th Cir. 1999).  Substantial evidence means “evidence adequate to support 
2   the jury’s conclusion, even if it is also possible to draw a contrary conclusion” from the same 
3   evidence.  Castro v. Cnty. of Los Angeles,
833 F.3d 1060, 1066
 (9th Cir. 2016) (internal quotation 
4   marks omitted).  “Thus, although the court should review the record as a whole, it must disregard 
5   evidence favorable to the moving party that the jury is not required to believe, and may not 
6   substitute its view of the evidence for that of the jury.”  Reeves,
530 U.S. at 151
.  In other words, 
7   entry of judgment as a matter of law is warranted only “if the evidence, construed in the light most 
8   favorable to the nonmoving party, permits only one reasonable conclusion, and that conclusion is 
9   contrary to the jury’s verdict.”  Castro,
833 F.3d at 1066
 (internal quotation marks omitted). 
10   B.   Motion for a New Trial                                           
11        Under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 59(a)(1), a court “may, on motion, grant a new trial 
12   on all or some of the issues.”  Fed. R. Civ. P. 59(a).  A trial court may grant a new trial, “even 
13   though the verdict is supported by substantial evidence, if the verdict is contrary to the clear 
14   weight of the evidence, or is based upon evidence which is false, or to prevent, in the sound 
15   discretion of the trial court, a miscarriage of justice.”  United States v. 4.0 Acres of Land,
175 F.3d 16 1133, 1139
 (9th Cir. 1999).  In considering a Rule 59(a) motion, the court “is not required to view 
17   the trial evidence in the light most favorable to the verdict.  Instead, the district court can weigh 
18   the evidence and assess the credibility of the witnesses.”  Experience Hendrix L.L.C. v. 
19   Hendrixlicensing.com Ltd.,
762 F.3d 829, 842
 (9th Cir. 2014).         
20        The decision to grant a new trial falls within the sound discretion of the trial court.  Kode v. 
21   Carlson,
596 F.3d 608, 611
 (9th Cir. 2010).  However, a court should not grant a new trial unless 
22   it is “left with the definite and firm conviction that a mistake has been committed.”  Landes 
23   Constr. Co. v. Royal Bank of Can.,
833 F.2d 1365, 1372
 (9th Cir. 1987) (internal quotations 
24   omitted).  A trial court’s determination that the verdict is not against the clear weight of the 
25   evidence is upheld unless there is an “absolute absence of evidence to support the jury’s verdict.”  
26   Hung Lam v. City of San Jose,
869 F.3d 1077, 1084
 (9th Cir. 2017) (internal quotation marks 
27   omitted) (emphasis in original).                                      
1   C.   Costs                                                            
2        Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 54(d)(1) provides that “[u]nless a federal statute, these 
3   rules, or a court order provides otherwise, costs—other than attorney’s fees—should be allowed to 
4   the prevailing party.”  Fed. R. Civ. P. 54(d)(1).  “Rule 54(d) creates a presumption in favor of 
5   awarding costs to prevailing parties, and it is incumbent upon the losing party to demonstrate why 
6   the costs should not be awarded.”  Stanley v. Univ. of S. Cal.,
178 F.3d 1069, 1079
 (9th Cir. 1999). 
7        Because of Rule 54(d)’s presumption in favor of awarding costs to a prevailing party, “a 
8   district court need not give affirmative reasons for awarding costs; instead, it need only find that 
9   the reasons for denying costs are not sufficiently persuasive to overcome the presumption in favor 
10   of an award.”  Save Our Valley v. Sound Transit,
335 F.3d 932, 945
 (9th Cir. 2003).  A court that 
11   declines to award costs, however, must justify its decision by explaining “why a case is not 
12   ‘ordinary’ and why, in the circumstances, it would be inappropriate or inequitable to award costs.”  
13   Ass’n of Mexican–Am. Educators v. State of Cal.,
231 F.3d 572, 593
 (9th Cir. 2000) (en banc); see 
14   Save Our Valley,
335 F.3d at 945
 (“A district court deviates from normal practice when it refuses 
15   to tax costs to the losing party, and that deviation triggers the requirement to ‘specify reasons.’”).   
16        Appropriate reasons for denying costs include: (1) the substantial public importance of the 
17   case, (2) the closeness and difficulty of the issues in the case, (3) the chilling effect on future 
18   similar actions, (4) the plaintiff's limited financial resources, and (5) the economic disparity 
19   between the parties.  Escriba v. Foster Poultry Farms, Inc.,
743 F.3d 1236
, 1247–48 (9th Cir. 
20   2014) (citing Ass’n of Mex.–Am. Educators, 231 F.3d at 592–93).  This is not “an exhaustive list 
21   of ‘good reasons’ for declining to award costs,” but rather a starting point for analysis.
Id.

27   causation in Plaintiff’s favor, even though Plaintiff has not presented any argument or evidence

6   Instructions at 15 (“Plaintiff must reasonably distinguish any security-price reaction to the

11        1.   Substantial Public Importance of the Case                   
12        When a case involves an issue of substantial public importance, it is a factor that weighs 
13   against awarding costs to the prevailing party.  Escriba, 743 F.3d at 1247–48.  There are multiple 
14   aspects to the present case which demonstrate that it involves an issue of substantial public 
15   importance.  First, according to Plaintiff, “[o]ver 22 million individuals” received the Tweets in 
16   which Mr. Musk announced that he had secured funding for the privatization transaction, and 
17   “hundreds of thousands, if not millions, of Tesla investors saw an impact to their financial 
18   portfolios.”  Docket No. 688 (Plaintiff’s Supplemental Briefing re Costs, or “Pl. Costs Br.”) at 2. 
19   Within days of the Tweets, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission promptly commenced 
20   investigations and filed lawsuits in response to Mr. Musk’s conduct, and news outlets published 
21   thousands of articles regarding the Tweets.  The barrage of media coverage leading up to and 
22   during the trial also reveals the public interest in the case.        
23        Second, this case is one of very few securities class actions to proceed to trial.  The 
24   Supreme Court has explained that “an animating purpose” of securities law is “to insure honest 
25   securities markets and thereby promote investor confidence.”  United States v. O’Hagan,
521 U.S. 26

544 U.S. 336, 345

743 F.3d at 1248
.  In Draper, the Ninth Circuit determined the case was close for two 
8   reasons: (1) plaintiff’s evidence of an Eighth Amendment violation was sufficient to survive 
9   summary judgment, and (2) the case ultimately turned on which competing account of events the 
10   jurors believed.  Draper v. Rosario,
836 F.3d 1072, 1088
 (9th Cir. 2016). 
11        Mr. Littleton’s case was close and presented difficult issues.  Last year, the Court granted 
12   partial summary judgment to Plaintiff on the issues of falsity and scienter, two elements of the 
13   Section 10b-5 claim.  See Summary Judgment Order at 32–33.  And as in Draper, the issue of 
14   materiality—one of the key issues in the case—turned in part on the jury’s assessment of witness 
15   credibility.  Draper,
836 F.3d at 1088
; see also Lopez v. Nguyen, No. 13-CV-3870-CRB, 2017
16 WL 512773
, at *2 (N.D. Cal. Feb. 8, 2017) (“Mr. Lopez’s case was close because his claims 
17   survived summary judgment as to four defendants and, as in Draper, turned on which witness 
18   testimony the jury believed.”).                                       
19        This factor thus also weighs against awarding costs to Defendants. 
20        3.   Chilling Effect on Future Similar Actions                   
21        If awarding costs may chill future similar actions, that too weighs against allowing their 
22   recovery by the prevailing party.  Escriba,
743 F.3d at 1248
.  Courts have found that taxing costs 
23   in labor and employment class actions would have a chilling effect on similar class actions.  See, 
24   e.g., Escriba, 743 F.3d at 1248–49 (refusing to award costs in FMLA case); Makaneole v. 
25   Solarworld Indus. Am., Inc., No. 14-cv-1528,
2017 WL 2345706
, at *3 (D. Or. May 10, 2017), 
26   report and recommendation adopted,
2017 WL 2345694
 (D. Or. May 30, 2017) (refusing to 
27   award costs in wage-claim litigation because of the “significant and undesirable chilling effect on 
1   4935972, at *1 (C.D. Cal. June 11, 2021) (declining to award costs in light of a chilling effect on 
2   future labor and employment class actions).                           
3        A securities fraud class action is not entirely analogous to a wage and hour class action.  
4   The high stakes and potential award may counter the chilling effect of an award of costs to the 
5   prevailing defendant.  Yet, as noted above, this case was complex and raised substantial issues.  
6   Under the circumstances of this case, this factor is neutral.         
7        4.   Financial Resources                                         
8        A party’s limited financial resources weighs against taxing costs.  Escriba,
743 F.3d at 9
   1248.  Although Plaintiff characterizes himself as a “semi-retired individual investor,” Plaintiff 
10   does not provide any specific information regarding his financial resources.  See Pl. Costs Br. at 4.  
11   Without providing information regarding Mr. Littleton’s financial situation, the Court is unable to 
12   determine whether or not paying Defendants’ costs would be a financial hardship.  See Courtney v. 
13   Oregon Dep’t of State Police, No. 06-cv-6223,
2008 WL 11408791
, at *2 (D. Or. Aug. 25, 2008) 
14   (ordering costs where there was “no proof of limited resources”); cf. Escriba,
743 F.3d at 1248
 
15   (declining to award costs where plaintiff “earned an average of $11,622 per year while working 
16   for Foster Farms, meaning that the costs being sought by the company exceed[ed] her average 
17   annual earnings”); Hamilton v. Yavapai Cmty. Coll. Dist., No. 12-cv-08193,
2022 WL 504474
, at 
18   *2 (D. Ariz. Feb. 18, 2022) (refusing to award costs where plaintiff provided information 
19   demonstrating that his average monthly expenses exceeded his income and that his family was 
20   financially dependent on him).                                        
21        Because Mr. Littleton has not demonstrated that paying Defendants’ costs would be a 
22   financial hardship, this factor weighs in favor of awarding costs.    
23        5.   Economic Disparity Between the Parties                      
24        The final factor turns on the economic disparity among the parties.  Escriba,
743 F.3d at 25
   1248.   While an economic disparity between the parties weighs against awarding costs, “it 
26   appears to be so only where the party seeking to avoid costs has limited financial resources and 
27   thus payment of costs would present a significant hardship.”  Jardin v. DATAllegro, Inc., No. 08-
1   Plaintiff has not pointed to any authority—nor has the Court independently been able to locate 
2   any—where an economic disparity between the parties weighed against awarding costs absent a 
3   corollary finding that the party had limited financial resources (despite the logic of considering 
4   economic disparity irrespective of the plaintiffs not being impoverished).  See
id.
 (awarding costs 
5   despite economic disparity where plaintiff “painted himself as a ‘highly successful database 
6   architecture inventor’”); cf. Lopez,
2017 WL 512773
, at *3 (citing examples where a party’s 
7   limited financial resources and economic disparity both weighed against awarding costs).   
8        Because it appears that an economic disparity between the parties only weighs against 
9   awarding costs where a party has limited financial resources and Mr. Littleton has not made this 
10   showing, this final factor weighs in favor of awarding costs.         
11        In sum: of the five Escriba factors, the first and second factors weigh against awarding 
12   costs, the third is neutral, and the fourth and fifth factors weigh in favor of awarding costs.  While 
13   a somewhat close call, in light of the substantial public importance of the case and the highly 
14   disputed issues of fact (with the Court having found falsity and scienter in favor of Plaintiffs prior 
15   to trial), the Court concludes that the Escriba factors are sufficient to overcome the presumption of 
16   awarding costs against Plaintiff in this case.  Each party shall bear their own costs.   
17                           V.    CONCLUSION                              
18        For the foregoing reasons, the Court DENIES Plaintiff’s motion for judgment as a matter 
19   of law and for a new trial.  The Court ORDERS each party to bear their own costs. 
20        This order disposes of Docket Nos. 657, 685, and 689.
